California
Fair POlitical
Practices Cornrnission
June 12, 1990
Tom vandeveld, III
Legal counsel
United Food and comrnercial workers
Local 135, AFL-cIo & cLc
2oo1 camino deI Rio South
San Diego, cA 92198
Re: Your Request for Advice
our FiIe No. A-90-255
Dear Mr. Vandeveld:
You have requested advice on behalf of the United Food and
Comrnercial workers Union Local 135 ('rLocal 135tt) and its federal
political 
action committee, the United Food and comrnercj.al workers
union, Local 135 Active BaIIot Conrnittee, concerning the campaign
provisions of the political 
Reform Act (the nAct ) . r The facts
itated in this Letter i.nclude j,nforrnation you provided me in
several telephone conversations prior to and since our receipt of
your letter.
OUESTIONS
1. Must the United Food and Cornmercial workers Union, Local
1,35 Active Ballot Cornnittee (the 'tCornnitteer') file a statenent of
organization with the Catifornia Secretary of State?
2. If so s/hat would be the Connittee's date of qualification
as a recipient comroittee?
3. Must the Conmittee file carnpaign statements for past
contributions to catifornia candidates and comrnittees?
4. Does the committee qualify as a 'ibroad based political
committeert? If so, when did it qualify?
5. If Local 135 decides to forn a slate mail-er organizatl'on,
v;hat reporting obligations must be met?
I 
Gor"..rment Code Sections 81ooo-910L5- AII statutory
references are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated.
commj.ssion regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations
Section 1,8OOO; et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Tit1e
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
^128 J Street, Strite 8!'tt) o P.O. Box 8O7 o Slcramcnto CA 958t14-t)Utr7 . (916) ]l:-i(,6(r


Vandeveld′  I■■′Tom
Page 2
6. If sLate mailers are paid for in part by candidates,
their. committees, barlot measuie eommittees, anil the slate mailer
organization, is there an aLlocation of nonie= .p"ri ;;t;;";
persons or ballot neasures appearing on the statl nailer?
CONCLUSION
1. The United Food and Cornmercial Workers Union Local 135
Active Batlot conmittee nust file a Recipient a".r,iat";-;Iltem"nt
of organization (Forn 410) with the cariiorni" s""i"["iy-oi 
st.t".
2. The Comrnittee should show July 1, 1998, as ridate
qualified as conmittee, rl
3. The conmittee is required to fire semi-annuar statements
reporting its activity 
conmencing with January 1,, 1988.
4. The Conmittee will qualify as a ,rbroad based political
committeerr if it rneets the ciiteril 
discussed in the rtrr-wing
analysis.
5 and 6. ff Local 135 receives or is prornised payments
totaling gs00 in a calendar year from candihates "; :;;;ila""=
that wish to appear _on a- slale mailer produced by tocai iaa, ".
from other persons for the purpose of iaving =p"'"i-i i"-"""aia.t"=
or measures included in such a slate nailer] L-oca1 135 "riii "rr"
gualify as a slate rnailer organization.
_ 
-Within 10 days of-gualifyilg as a slate mailer organization,
Local- 135 nust fire a slate ttailer organization staten6ni or
organization (Forn 4oo). rn addition] srate rnairei orlinizations
nust fiLe serui-annual, pre-election, and late payment i"poit=
disclosing payments received and expenditures iii"-t; 
;;il;""
slate rnailers.
., 
Yld:. the Act, a- slate nailer organization that produces and
ctrstributes slate mailers is not considered to be mak-ing
contributions or independent expenditures to or on behal-f of the
candidates or comrnittees supporLed in the mailer. tfr"i"i"i",
there. is no duty inposed on the slate nailer organizati"" i"
prorate palments received from the various persons or entities who
pay for the maiLers. However, an organizatlon like Loca1 135
which sponsors a recipient conmittee and also quarifies as a slate
mai-Ier organization must take precautions to aioid vior-ation of
the contribution linitations. 
For example, any pa)rments made by
Local 135 or the cornmittee at the behest or a Lanaiaate for the
purpose of having the candidate incl.uded in a slate mailer wilr be
contributions to the candidate, In addition, if there i= u.ry
understanding between Locar r-35 or the cornmii.tee and a candidate
that other candidates or conruittees wirr be asked to participate
in the slate mailer in order to cover the costs for that
candidate, LocaL 135 and the committee rnay be deemed to be making
contributions to the candidate.


Vandeveld′  III′ Tom
page 3
FACTS
Un■ted Food and Commerc■
a■ Workers
which was formed in 
■986 as a result of
and Co■llnercia■ Workers Union Loca■
 ■222
Commercia■ Workers Union Loca■
 229A.
Local 135 is a labor union
a merger of United Food
and United Food and
Prior to 1984, Local 1222 forr0,ed a recipient cornmittee to
make contributions to state and Local candidates, and a statement
of Organization (Form 410) was filed. 
In 1985, this comrnittee was
terminated and a staternent of Terrnination (Forn 4l-5) was filed.
on June 14, 1984, the United Food and commercial Workers
Union LocaL 1222 Active Bal1ot colTrnittee was forrned to make
contributions to federal candidates. A statement of organization
was filed with the Federal Elections Cornmission. Funding for the
Comrnittee cornes in the forn of voluntary contributions rather than
rnembership dues. In addition to making independent expenditures
and contributions to or on behaff of various federal candidates
and one other federal cornrnittee, the Connittee has also nade
contributions to various state and tocal candidates. The first
contribution from the Cornrnittee to a California state or 1ocaI
candidate was nade on September 25, L9a6. subsequently, a
contribution of $3,000 vras made on JuIy 1, l-988, to Assembl)rnan
Peter chacon. Since then, additi.onal contributions have been made
to california candidates from committee funds between January 1,
1989 and August 25, 1989.
In addition, bet\.reen July 1, 1989 and December 31, 1989,
Local 135 made independent expenditures to support a California
city council candidate. This was from the union's general
treasury fund which receives its noney from rnenbers' dues.
ANALYSIS
1 - 3. Any person or combination of Persons which receives
contributions of $1,ooo or more in a calendar year qualifies under
the Act as a rrrecipient committee. t' (Section 8201-3(a).)
contributions are defined in the Act as pa)rnents for I'political
purposes.r' (Section 82015.) Regulation 18215 (copy enclosed)
ctaiities that a palment is made for political 
purposes if it is:
(1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting
to influence the action of the voters for or against the
nomination or election of a candi.date or candidates, or
the qualification or passage of any measure; or


Vandeve■d′  III′ Tom
Page 5
determine the amount of contribution. 
Factors relevant
to a determination that the donor has reason to knor.,
that all or part of the palrment will be used to make
expenditures and contributions include but are not
linited to the established practice of the person or
organization with respect to expenditures and
contributions and any representations made Lrhen the
Payment is solicited.
The Cornmission has advised that a federal committee which has
not notified its contributors that it wiII use its funds to make
contributions or expenditures to support or oppose Cal-ifornia
state or loca1 candidates or cornmittees, will qualify as a
recipient conmittee under Section 82013 (a) during the second year
in which it engages in such activities. 
That is because any
contributions and expenditures Dade during the first calendar year
in which the committee engages in such activitj.es establish a
rrhistory't that puts the conrnittee's contributors on notice that
some or all of their funds may be used for political 
purposes in
California. 
If the committee makes contributions or other
expenditures for political 
purposes in California totalling 
$1,000
or more during any subsequent calendar year, it is deemed to have
"received" contributions total-1ing $1,o00 or more. (Enclosed is
an information sheet which describes how federal cornmittees and
other organizations that do not receive contributions specif i.caIly
for california elections nay qualify as recipj.ent comnittees in
California. )
The conmittee has nade several contributions to CaLifornia
state and local candidates and coumittees. The first contribution
was made on September 25, ]-986. This contribution established a
history of using Conmittee funds for rrpolitical purposes.rr The
next contribution nade to a California state or local candidate or
comnittee was for $3,000 on JuIy 1, 1988. At this point, the
conmittee is deemed under Regulation 18215(c) to have received
contributions totaling $3,o0o which gualified it as a California
recipient cornmittee pursuant to Section 82013(a). Section 84101
requires a recipient committee to file rrith the Secretary of State
a Statement of Organization (Fom 41O) rrithin 10 days after it has
qualified as a conmittee. Therefore, the committee should have
filed a Form 41O no later than July 11, 1988.
Disclosure statements must be filed to reflect aII
contributions received and expenditures made once an organization
qualifies as a cornrnittee. The Committee did not qualify as a
committee in 1986. Therefore, the Conmittee had no filing
requirement in 1986. However, since it did qualify as a recipient
conmittee in 1988, in addition to the Statement of Organization
(Forn 410), it must file appropriate campaign disclosure
statements (Form 420 or Form 450) covering from January 1, L988


Vandeveld, III, Tom
Page 4
(2) Received by or made at the behest of:
(A) A candidate, unless it is clear from
surrounding circumstances that the payment was
received or nade at his or her behest for personal
purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or
status as an office holder. The term rrpaymentrr
includes the candidate's own money or property used
on behalf of his or her candidacy;
(B) A controlled committee,'
(c) An official 
committee of a politica}
party, including a state central cornnittee, county
central conrnittee, assernbly district 
conmittee or
any subcommittee of such committeel or
(D) An organization formed or existj,ng
prinarj-Iy for political 
purposes as defined j.n
subsection (a) (1), including but not linj-ted to a
political 
action committee established by any
membership organization, Iabor union or
corporation.
(b) 
rrMade at the behestrr means a payment made
under the control or at the direction of, in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert
with, or at the request or suggestion of a candidate,
controll-ed cornmittee, official 
cornmittee of a political
party, or organization forrned or existing prinarily for
political 
purposes.
(c) ttContributiontr includes any monetary or non-
monetary pa)q[ent for which fuIl and adequate consider-
ation is not made to the donor that is received by any
person or organization other than a candidate,
controlled comnittee, official 
cornmittee of a political
party, or organization formed or existing primariLy for
political 
purposes if the payment is rrearmarked'r for the
rnaking of contributions or expenditures. A paynent is
I'earmarkedrr when, at the tine of making the pa)rment, the
donor knows or has reason to know that the payment or
funds with which the palment will be conningled will 
be
used to nake contributions, as defined in covernment
code section 82015 and this regulation, or expenditures,
as defined in Government Code Section 82025 and 2 CaI.
Adn. Code Section La225. If the donor knows or has
reason to know that only part of the payment will be
used to nake contributions or expenditures, the payment
shall be apportioned on a reasonable basis in order to


Vandeve■d′  III′ Tom
Page 6
through December 31, 1989.2
The Cornmittee may file 
one campaign
statement for the entire year January 1, 1988 through December 31,
1988. For 1989, the conmittee should file two semi-annual
statements covering January 1 - June 30, 1989, and JuIy 1 -
December 31, L989. whether the cordmittee files Form 420 or Form
450 is determined by rrhether it has received contributions
totalling 
$10o or more during the calendar year from one
contributor. 
If it did receive $1o0 or more from one contributor,
you must f il-e Form 420, otherrrise you may use the more abbreviated
Form 45o. An organization which uses only a portion of the funds
it receives for naking contributions or expenditures to support or
oppose California candidates or baIlot measures is required to
report as 'rcontributions receivedtr only that portion of its
receipts which are actually spent in connection with supporting or
opposing California state or local candidates or ballot neasures.
The enclosed information sheet will assist you in deterrnining how
to report the contributions received.
4. Regulation 18502 (copy enclosed) provides that a
comrnittee qualifies as a 'rbroad based political 
cornmi-ttee'r vrhen it
meets alL of the following requirernents: it has existed as a
california recipient committee for more than six months; in the
period covered by the current fiscal year and the tvro previous
fiscal years it has received contributions from Loo or more
contributors; and in the current fiscal year and the two previous
fiscal years it has made contributions to five or more candidates.
5 and 6. An entity qualifies as a slate rnailer organization
if it receives or is promised $500 or nore in a calendar year for
the purpose of producing one or more slate nailers r.rhich support
or oppose four or nore candidates or ballot measures. (Sections
82048.3 and 82048.4.) If the Conmittee decides to forrn a slate
mailer organization, it nust file a Statenent of organization
(Forn 4o0) within 10 days of qualifying as a slate mailer
organization. If an entity qualifies as a slate mailer
organization during the last 16 days before an election for which
it is required to file preelection statements, the organization
rnust file the infornation required to be reported in the Slate
Mailer Statement of Organization within 24 hours of qualifying as
a slate mailer organization. The information must be filed by
telegram or personal delivery. 
In addition, the Form 400 must be
filed within 10 days of qualifying as indicated above.
(Section 84108 . )
' 
Section 91013 specifies that any person filing 
an original
statement after any deadline sha11, in addition, to any otfrer
penalties or remedies, be liable in the amount of ten dollars per
day after the deadline until the statement is filed. 
No Iiability
under this section shall exceed the cunul,ative amount stated in
the Iate statement or report, or one hundred dollars, whichever is
greater .


Vandeve■d, III′ Tom
page 7
Enclosed is an information manual for slate maiLer
organizations which sets out in detail the filing obligations of a
slate mailer organization.
If ne can be of any further assistance please do not hesitate
to contact ne at (915) 322-5662.
Sincerely,
scott HaIlabrin
Political 
Ref orrn Consultant
Enc losures
Actり
           
し
By: Wayrle P. Irnberi
